result: I keep thinking about the way light looks when it’s almost gone—how it clings to the edges of things like it’s afraid to leave. Yesterday evening the neighbor’s maple held a last ember of sun in its highest leaves, a color that doesn’t have a name because it lasts for only three heartbeats. I stood on the porch with tea gone cold and tried to memorize it, knowing I’d fail. Memory always dims the chroma, turns specific gold into a generic “nice sunset.” Still, I try. Maybe that’s what we’re all doing: collecting brief, untitled colors before the dark makes them equal. Inside, the kitchen radio played a song I pretended to recognize; the bass felt like someone else’s pulse in my wrist. I wondered how many people were listening at that exact moment, how many of us were pretending the lyrics were written only for the inside of our particular skulls. The dog nosed my ankle, reminding me that dinner happens whether transcendence shows up or not. I poured kibble, scratched the soft place behind her ear where the fur is thinned like velvet on an old couch arm. Later, I opened the refrigerator just to watch the light inside—artificial, dependable, willing to stay on all night if asked. It didn’t care what I’d lost or found. It just glowed, steady and unashamed of not being sunset. I closed the door, left it shining for no one, and felt strangely comforted by its pointless loyalty to empty shelves.
